CHAPTER 9 — SUBAGENTS: KEEPING THE BUILD CLEAN

Chapter 9 — Subagents: Keeping the Build — A subagent is a Claude session that runs in its own context window and reports back a summary. When your grading tool's
NARRATION

This chapter gives a working overview of Subagents: Keeping the Build Clean, focusing on the ideas a reader needs before moving to the next chapter. The chapter moves through Learning outcomes, Opening, What a subagent is, Subagents vs. Skills vs. /clear, and related ideas. Read it for the main argument, the vocabulary it introduces, and the practical judgment it asks you to develop. A subagent is a Claude session that runs in its own context window and reports back a summary.

// SLIDE 02 — OPENING

OPENING

78%
of the window
→
30%
of the window
→
22%
was barely enough to draft feedback for one student before t
NARRATION

The teacher's grading-tool session had been running for an hour. The narrowing principle from Chapter 8 was in place , Claude was detecting patterns; the teacher would write feedback from the flags. The Skill from Chapter 6 was active. The Hooks from Chapter 7 were enforcing the never-rules. The teacher needed one more thing before drafting per-student feedback: a quick investigation of how the school's existing tools handled late submissions. The school had a policy.

// SLIDE 03 — WHAT A SUBAGENT IS

WHAT A SUBAGENT IS.

·Its own context window. Independent of the main session. The subagent can read 30 files; the main session sees only the
·Its own system prompt. Specialized for the subagent's role (a researcher, a reviewer, a pattern analyzer). The system pr
Its own allowed tools. The subagent's permissions can be tighter than the main session's. A research subagent might haveand no Write or Edit. The narrower permissions are a safety layer.
·A short summary as its output. The main session prompts the subagent; the subagent runs; the subagent returns a structur
·The directory containing submissions (one subdirectory per student).
NARRATION

A subagent is a Claude session spawned by the main session. It has: Its own context window. Independent of the main session. The subagent can read 30 files; the main session sees only the subagent's final summary. Its own system prompt. Specialized for the subagent's role (a researcher, a reviewer, a pattern analyzer). The system prompt lives in the subagent's definition file. Its own allowed tools. The subagent's permissions can be tighter than the main session's.

// SLIDE 04 — SUBAGENTS VS. SKILLS VS. /CLEA

SUBAGENTS VS. SKILLS VS. /CLEAR

Subagent.→Skill.→/clear.
NARRATION

Three context-management tools, three roles. Subagent. Delegates work to an isolated context. Use when the task has a clear input/output shape and the per-task work would otherwise bloat the main session. The subagent runs separately; its work does not enter the main context.

// SLIDE 05 — THE FOUR MOST USEFUL SUBAGENT

THE FOUR MOST USEFUL SUBAGENT PATTERNS FOR TEACHERS.

Investigate something. Return findings only.

The four most useful subagent patterns f — Example: "Use a research subagent to investigate how to handle late submissions across the school's late-submission poli
NARRATION

Investigate something. Return findings only. Example: "Use a research subagent to investigate how to handle late submissions across the school's late-submission policy doc, the LMS export, and the November meeting notes. Return a three-paragraph summary of the operative rules including the documented exceptions."

// SLIDE 06 — A WORKED EXAMPLE: PATTERN ANAL

A WORKED EXAMPLE: PATTERN ANALYSIS ON THE GRADING WORKFLOW

32%
| Fibonacci off-by-one | 5 | 20%
→
16%
| Missing docstrings | 12 | 48%
·Base case omitted in factorial (8 submissions). Students wrote
·Off-by-one in fibonacci's base cases (5 submissions). Students
·Use of global state in helper functions (4 submissions). Students
NARRATION

Walk through the full pattern from the grading-tool's perspective. Step 1: write the subagent definition. The pattern-analyzer.md above goes in .claude/agents/. Commit to git.

// SLIDE 07 — TEACHER BUILD: A SUBAGENT FOR

TEACHER BUILD: A SUBAGENT FOR YOUR GRADING WORKFLOW

·Write the pattern-analyzer subagent. Use the worked example above; adapt for your subject and rubric. Commit to git.
Invoke for your current assignment's submissions. The first run is the test. Verify the subagent stays inside its permisno Writes).
·Compare context usage with and without the subagent. Run a short session inline (read three submissions in the main sess
Optionallywrite a reviewer subagent for the Writer/Reviewer pattern. Used to review your grading-tool code (or other build code) f
·Add to CLAUDE.md. A short note about which subagents the project uses, when to invoke each, what they return.
NARRATION

Apply the chapter's discipline to your grading tool. Write the pattern-analyzer subagent. Use the worked example above; adapt for your subject and rubric. Commit to git.

// SLIDE 08 — GATE 2: ACT TWO ACCEPTANCE

GATE 2: ACT TWO ACCEPTANCE

·CLAUDE.md spans two build tiers. Class-website (Act One) plus grading tool (Act Two). The Lessons Learned section has at
One classroom activity per Act Two concept designedcapacities (Chapter 5), Skills (Chapter 6), Hooks (Chapter 7), dangerous middle (Chapter 8), subagents (Chapter 9). Five
NARRATION

Act Two closes here. The acceptance criteria: Working grading tool built through the framework: Chapter 4 handoff conditions, Chapter 5 capacity prep, Chapter 6 Skill, Chapter 7 Hooks, Chapter 8 narrowing principle, Chapter 9 subagent.

// SLIDE 09 — CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS D

CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS DESIGN AND USE A SUBAGENT

·A research subagent for a topic they need to investigate.
·A pattern-analysis subagent for a dataset.
·A linter or test-runner subagent with narrow permissions.
·Frontmatter with name, description, allowed tools.
·System prompt that names the input, the workflow, the output format.
NARRATION

Each student writes a subagent definition for a specific task in a project they are working on: A research subagent for a topic they need to investigate. A pattern-analysis subagent for a dataset. A linter or test-runner subagent with narrow permissions.

// SLIDE 10 — COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

"Subagent = another Claude tab."→"Subagents are for complex builds only."→"More subagents = better."→"The Writer/Reviewer pattern is overkill."→"Auto memory across subagents is magic."
NARRATION

"Subagent = another Claude tab." No. The subagent is spawned from the main session; it runs in its own context; only the summary returns. There is no second window to manage. "Subagents are for complex builds only." Even a single grading-tool session benefits from delegating the pattern-analysis. The criterion is would this work otherwise bloat the main context?

// SLIDE 11 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

Herbert Simon (1916–2001) — American polymath, Nobel laureate in economics, Turing Award winner, founding figure of arti

AI Wayback Machine — A subagent is Simon's nearly decomposable subsystem applied to Claude Code. The pattern-analyzer's inside is detailed: 2
NARRATION

Herbert Simon (1916, 2001) , American polymath, Nobel laureate in economics, Turing Award winner, founding figure of artificial intelligence and cognitive science, whose 1962 paper "The Architecture of Complexity" formalized the principle of nearly decomposable systems: that large complex systems are best understood and operated upon as clusters of subsystems that are tightly connected internally and loosely connected externally.
